description: A made opening that reaches groundwater, together with the structure that keeps it usable, is a 'phitura'. 'thula' (spring) is where the water reaches the surface naturally. Whether a well is shared, privately held, or safe to drink from remains part of its local account.
articulatory_notes: Narrow the lips for unvoiced bilabial 'ph' and let the breath enter high-front unrounded 'i'. Place the tongue tip against the backs of the upper teeth for unvoiced dental 't' and release into stressed high-back rounded 'u'. Tap or briefly trill the tongue at the alveolar ridge, then open into central 'a'.
sound_symbolism: Opening 'phi' recalls 'phialu' (water), then the dental stop and tap give the word two clear contacts before final 'a'. The family resemblance is a mnemonic, not a productive way to derive other water structures.
usage_notes: The English gloss distinguishes this noun from 'towe' (well, in good health). Water source and depth can be stated separately from construction. Access and test results take their own clauses when they matter.

examples.translation: The child carries water from the well.

pillars.solarpunk-values: A well makes groundwater available through continuing maintenance. Its usefulness also depends on energy, access, and water quality.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Siting and lining a well require accumulated knowledge of local ground and water. So do covering, drawing from, and cleaning it. A dependable source is maintained across generations.

semantic_domains.community: a water source whose access and upkeep may be organised among people
semantic_domains.creation: a made opening and structure for reaching groundwater
semantic_domains.spatial: a fixed access point connecting the surface to water below
